segments.text: Need help?
You hold that.
One
moment.
No. Very tight. Tēnā koutou katoa. Tēnā koe, Minister.
Tēnā koe.
Kia ora koutou.
Thank you very much for coming in front of us again.
We had our opportunity to cover Whānau
Ora yesterday. Today,
we are going across
numerous other parts of Māori
development.
And just so that we're aware, we've actually got
programmed a couple of breaks during this session.
Yes.
The first break will be at approximately 10:30, and then we'll
have another one at approximately 11:30.
That might sound a little bit short in between, but we
will aim for that there as the outcome.
But to begin with, Minister, we'll
provide you with five minutes to provide some introductory comments
before we get into the questioning.
Ae. Mihi ana kia tatou e whakatau nei te
kaupapa o te ata. Ara, te aroha hai hai ki nga
mahi a Te Puni Kōkiri. I anahi.
Ko ta te korero mo te kaupapa, te hotaka,
me te pūtea whakatuwi i a Whānau Ora.
A, a te ra nei he kaupapa anō kei te haere.
Na reira, tēnā tatou katoa. And thank you everyone for coming, and thank you for
your probing questions yesterday and the intelligence that you brought to the
conversation.
Thank you very much.
You certainly raised mine along the way.
It wasn't enough.
Te Puni Kōkiri, as we know, is enabled through the Ministry of Māori
Development Act 1991, and some very clear
purposes
for Te Puni Kōkiri and functions as set out in Section 5
of that legislation. And in August
2024, Cabinet endorsed
my approach, a modernized approach to Māori development and to
position Te Puni Kōkiri for strategic influence.
I'm joined here by the Secretary for Māori Development, Dave Samuels, and
Steve Sutton, along with other RPHR and officials from Te Puni
Kōkiri.
And that positioning is more around ensuring that we're
very clear on the role and the responsibilities of Te Puni
Kōkiri. Within that rubric and the vision of thriving Whānau,
but to ensure that it leads policy advice to the government on
Māori development,
and also iwi and Māori Crown relations.
It has a very firm monitoring and mentoring role.
It takes
leadership around the government's engagement on culture and language,
and importantly, Māori economic development and growth.
So we sort of
encapsulate or characterize Te Puni Kōkiri as having five strategic
priority areas. One we covered off yesterday, Whānau Ora.
Then there's monitoring and mentoring, economic development and growth, culture and
language, and iwi
Māori Crown relations. And I'm sure if we engage
on those issues as we go through today, it could be a more
structured and meaningful conversation for us all, rather than jumping all over
the place. If you want to do that too, I can do that.
I used to do gymnastics once down in Otahuhu for two-door tumblers, and that was
awesome. But if you just want to do one subject at a time that might take
forty-five minutes, that's fine.
So in terms of Māori language and culture, a lot of
talk, a lot of korero,
but you'll be aware that there's been some additional pūtea that's been set aside
as a contingency
for Māori broadcasting, which I'm sure the Honorable Willie Jackson
will be very proud of me for doing alongside the Minister of
Finance. In addition to that, we've also
reallocated
$10 million over five years to reincarnate
and reestablish the Te Māori kaupapa under the rubric Te Māori 2,
which is effectively showcasing our culture and arts to the world through
museum and other interactions. But backing that up with Māori economic
development, things like film, fashion, music, agribusiness, manufacturing, and
everything else at expo into place.
That place will be Europe and it may be some other places as well.
As many of you will know, Te Māori was a raging success forty years
ago, and I think we can emulate that and actually do better,
by having a firm focus on culture as a platform and a
springboard for economic
development and economic growth and diplomatic engagement as
well.
So, the monitoring and mentoring role, we are really focused on lifting
system accountability for Māori outcomes and service adequacy
by constantly identifying, tracking, and carrying forward
issues on the adequacy of the other agencies throughout
government to actually deliver on what they're supposed to deliver on, which is
better outcomes for all citizens, Māori in particular
for Te Puni Kōkiri.
And
where needed, calibration around the responsibilities that the Crown has
vis-à-vis iwi. So making sure that there's a clear
delineation between those kaupapa or matters that
are iwi in orientation,
and those kaupapa matters which are Māori in orientation.
And that's why we have a policy framework known as Te Tōtūhe Ora that has now
been
shared,
and mentored throughout the public service.
I think we had seven agencies that are now in, let's call it training
mode
around Te Tōtūhe Ora.
And making sure that Te Puni Kōkiri is
positioned more like a central agency, that it can
hold other agencies to account,
and ensure that the system is performing better for Māori.
Now, a great example of that, of course, is the government targets,
and we have been very deliberate and we've got a great team working on data and
monitoring led by Adie and others.
I don't know if Adie's here or she- And we should give her the final thing.
Who are very deliberate about making sure that there are
appropriate inquiries made on the delivery on the
government targets vis-à-vis Māori. So it's not just all New Zealanders.
We have a real sense of where things are going well across the eight social
government targets, not the climate change target, but the eight others.
Whether or not serious young youth offenders, or attendance at
school, or
wait times at emergency departments.
We have a sense on how that's going for Māori.
And we also have some additional reporting, which we think is really
important around the improvement of
achievement by Māori across those various spheres that are
outlined in Section 5 [1]
of the Ministry of Māori Development Act, health, education, employment and
training, economic development.
So a very data-driven approach to ensuring that
the monitoring and mentoring work that Te Puni Kōkiri undertakes is not only
credible but enduring and of the nature that other
agencies know they need to come to Te Puni Kōkiri or engage with Te Puni Kōkiri or
watch out, Dave is on you, on your job.
In terms of Māori economic development, there are a variety of
pathways for
Te Puni Kōkiri to activate in this space.
One of them, which we released two weeks ago, is around the Papakāinga National
Environmental State Standard,
which we announced alongside the Ministry for the Environment to enable
up to 10 homes to be built on Māori land blocks without having to
kowtow and run around trying to fill in the red tape at Council.
That means basically less or no resource consent requirements
for Māori landowners to build 10 homes on Māori
land blocks. Now, that is a massive game changer.
And all you have to do, as I mentioned yesterday, is talk with Jackson Tutahi,
TikTok from Hamilton,
who's been putting together a range of initiatives around developing Papakāinga
throughout the country to know that that is a huge step to
enable more economic development and more social and
cultural development on our whenua Māori.
We also have a range of initiatives that were announced last week around supporting
Māori in agribusiness, in the primary sector, support for the Ākato King Salmon
business, which is owned by the Ōnukurū Ngāi Ngāte Pō and others.
The PKW Corporation around energy resilience and building a solar array on the
Waimate Plains in South Taranaki, and also Ngāti Hineuru who are
putting in some
upgrades to their cherry orchards in Te Matau a Māui.
So the Māori Development Fund continues, alongside some
housing investment through Papakāinga, through Sortit Kāinga Ora
programs and repairs,
and
other community resilience initiatives that often draw on the Māori
Development Fund in order to make sure our communities are safe and well.
Finally, the Iwi Māori Crown Relations portfolio.
There's a number of matters that we can describe and refer to, like the
advancement of the Māori Purposes Bill.
Actually, there's two bills that are moving forward.
The improvement in the implementation of treaty settlements and the
decrease in delivery issues that are arising from
outstanding treaty settlement obligations.
That's come down from 3.5% to 1.9% of all the
14,800 obligations there are.
The responses to the whole of system call Crown report
on treaty settlement delivery. Some of those gnarly issues that often pop up
in treaty settlement discussions, sometimes things were missed in the
settlements
documentation and the legislation, and Te Puni Kōkiri has a role and a function to
go and help tidy that up. It's really post-settlement stuff rather than
pre-settlement stuff, which is the purview of Minister Goldsmith and Te Tai
Tokerau.
And of course, something that's very close and near to my heart,
which is the progress resolution of the claims of
thousands of owners known as the Step and Walker to Nelson
tense
fiduciary claims on the top of the South Island.
So overall, positioning Te Puni Kōkiri for strategic influence.
We talked about Whānau Ora yesterday.
Today we can, I think, talk about those other four areas and of course, the hot
topic, which is the putia and the budget appropriation and allocation, which I
know Steve has been staying up late at night
combing through all the data to ask me the hard questions.
Let's go.
Thank you, Minister. Just for the agenda that we have
for us, could I just ask that housing, whenua Māori
and Māori broadcasting and language, should we leave that for this afternoon?
We'll concentrate on all the other stuff-
All right
... this morning. Okay. So, one of the first questions to you.
Oh, yeah.
So we'd all agree, I think, in this room that in the
employment, health, and housing area, Māori are probably
more in need than anyone else.
That sort of comes up all the time, doesn't it?
So given that, Minister,
we've seen a decrease of 190 million since
2023 in terms of Māori development.
How do you
front Māori,
and how do you validate that, justify that when your own department is down
190 million since 2023, given the need
in terms of employment, housing,
and health? That's the first question.
Second one, just if you want to roll off that.
Is this government still committed to a
by Māori for Māori approach? I think that's the question that's
coming from Māori.
If you are, why is initiatives and targets for Māori being
sidelined, being skipped over,
being thrown out? So just a couple of easy questions for you
at the start.
Oh, they were like low balls like that Australian- ...
New Zealand cricket match many years ago. Okay.
Sorry about that.
So let's go.
By Māori for Māori- Absolutely. And that's why we're going
hard across various portfolios, including housing, to ensure
that those relationships around housing continue, and
people build into the programs that have been set up by the previous government and
also by this government around the Maori housing portfolios.
Now, under the previous government, there are about 1,050 homes that were
contracted under the Whāikainga Wairanga program.
There's about 750 that have been built with CCCs.
There's 300 to go. That's three years later.
Under this government, it's about 900 to 1,000.
About 100 have reached CCC. There's about 900 to go just
under the Whāikainga Wairanga . So we've still got probably two, three,
maybe four years of build
runway to ensure that that continues.
In addition to that, some of the Maori housing providers that were
supported and curated under the auspicious leadership of Minister
Peeni Henare and with scaffolding from Minister Jackson have now turned
into strategic partners for the community housing provision, the Budget
24 1,500 homes that were contracted
by the Ministry of Housing Urban Development.
So for example, yesterday I went out to Porirua, Tuki Street, 10
houses of the 125 homes that are being built by Te
Akoru Moawhio, which is a Ngati Toa Rangatira entity that builds into the
1,500 homes announced through Budget 24
for community housing providers.
By Maori for Maori, and actually in some instances, for everyone.
So that's a great example, in my view, where we've started a
program,
and it was started under the previous government, acknowledged, continue with that
program, and actually it's broadened out as well.
And that's something, and a type of whakatauki that my former
chairperson, James Brown, Hemi Taonui,
singer, dancer, movie star, would say, by Maori for Maori, for everyone.
So that's a great example. But when I front
into Maori communities, I say, "Is it better than it was in
2023?" Treaty settlements. Yes. And
under the history and history legacy of the national-led governments, a
lot more treaty settlements have been curated. Treaty settlements implementation.
Yes, it's better now than it was in 2023.
In the last year it's gone from 83% to 86% or less delivery issues.
Two-year-old immunization rates, which is a hot topic of mine, which was 89% in
2017, went to 63.5% in 2023. Now it's back over
65. If there's one thing that we need to sort out, it's make sure our kids are
immunized. Serious violent crime.
Yes, less more Maori victims of serious violent crime. Serious youth offenders.
Yes, decreased significantly and materially.
Emergency housing. Georgie knows, in Hamilton we had 756
kids, most of whom were Maori, living in that defunct
dank dark arrangement emergency housing. Now there's about 60.
Most of those kids have moved out. In fact, nearly 700,
many of whom were Maori. School attendance has improved.
Would we like to see it better? Absolutely.
ED states, yes, it has improved marginally for Maori.
Elective wait times, yes, it has improved marginally for Maori.
Transfers of properties of significance like Korotipu Pa, Orako, and other
properties. Yes, we actually did that, sorted that. Farming.
You can't argue that Maori farmers are doing better under the current arrangements.
Taxation. Minister Willis in Budget '24 ensured that $400
million of taxpayers' money, your money, stayed in Whanau Maori hands
and wasn't sucked up by an enlarging government. Roading.
Absolutely. If you want to fix a road between Gisborne and Opotiki to enable many
Maori to go and visit their whanau for Tangihanga and hui, absolutely.
Inflation, interest rates. Do we think that those affect Maori? Yes, we do.
Free trade agreements, the same. Maori Development Fund, Regional Infrastructure
Fund, fast track projects. 150 projects on the second schedule,
25 of which were owned by Maori. The first or the second one was a Maori-owned
project down in the South Island. Is that better? Yes. Okay.
Accords. Are we delivering on the accords? Yes, we are.
Papakainga, NEES, Greenfields, cancer drugs, tertiary enrollment.
I could range over 20, 40, 60 indicators of
improvement for Maori communities and Maori individuals.
And you know what? The data speaks volumes.
Righty-o. Thank you.
Thank you, Robert.
Thank you. We'll come back to the question.
That was a very broad question with a very broad answer.
That's right. Out of respect to the minister, I let him waffle on.
As I said, questions can accept answers.
Nowhere down the track, nowhere in that response did he actually answer the
question.
No, by far, by Maori .
And then you thought we were on housing-
Yeah
... but now we're not on housing, and can I just come back to the question,
which is, and maybe I'll put it to the officials, given since the
minister's incapable of giving a coherent response.
But I'll ask the
CEO. You're $190 million down, Dave,
over the last three years. This minister's given away $190 million
of Maori money in a time where the need for
Maori has never been higher in terms of jobs,
health,
homes. Never been higher, and your department has gone backwards.
I would like from you, since the minister's unable to do it, to give me a
response of why we have had to give away
so much money and why the department, despite what the minister's saying,
is struggling so much at the moment.
Oh.
Well, that's for Dave. I wonder.
That's for me.
Since the minister can't answer it.
No, no, I can answer that. And then I'll pass it to Dave.
Will you try to attempt to answer the question this time?
I'll answer that again for you, because I don't think you heard the previous
answer.
No, I did.
But I'll answer that again for you.
The role and the responsibility of the Ministry for Maori Development is well set
out in a statute, the strategic intentions document, and by way of a cabinet
decision that was made, is to position itself for strategic influence and
not be a DMA type entity, which is what maybe
you and others want it to be.
190 million. Why'd you give it away?
What others want it to be. We absolutely have to make sure that the
government and government agencies which can basically
manage- 99.5% of the entire budget, which is not in Te Puni
Kōkiri, are responsive to the needs of Māori.
If we think that Te Puni Kōkiri is the best entity, for example, to reduce the
serious youth offender rate amongst Māori, or to
massively and materially change the two-year-old immunization
rates amongst Māori kids, or to run
down the treaty settlement process pre-settlement, then we're
confusing the role of Te Puni Kōkiri.
Te Puni Kōkiri is not the Department of Māori Affairs.
It's just not. So we have to be absolutely articulate in defining
and making sure that the role of Te Puni Kōkiri is elevated to more
than a cottage program manager, which is
not what it is. Now, there have been instances where that's gone very well.
For example, Whānau Ora. Listen, we talked about this yesterday.
Success, and now we're going to build on it and make it even better. Okay?
But there are some things, actually, it's better that we ensure that other
agencies which have the bulk of the budget, the bulk of the staff, the bulk of the
regulatory empowerment, perform for Māori.
I agree.
We have to make sure that that is done in a material and a
defensible and a credible way. Because we can't say, "Oh, it's okay.
All Māori issues have to be managed and implemented and programmed out of Te Puni
Kōkiri." We don't have the budget.
No.
We don't.
Because you-- well, you gave it all away, so.
No. No. And-
190 million.
What I also say is that I do not accept the
characterization of Tama or Dave giving away the money.
But what we've done is we've elevated the role.
All agencies, bar about five or six, have had to give up some
budget.
I-- thank you. We've got to the-
So health, education, defense, law and order
We've got to the resource.
All other agencies have had to give up something.
But at the same breath, we've also had some wins.
We've had ups and downs. One of them, of course, there's an issue dear to your
heart, Māori broadcasting. Thank you.
So I want to ask this question. Is that fair?
That's what I want to ask the TPK-
Yes
... team. I want to ask this question.
Given Māori
development
TPK, I should say, is the most underfunded and under-resourced government
department. Given that, you have a think about that,
Tama. Go and have a Google something.
But-
Just a giggle or Google
... if you want to go back, you want to go back 20 years.
No, because you're right, Minister. You talked about-- you're quite right.
Māori
TPK is about policy development. There was a time when Māori
Affairs was looking after everything. You know this, though.
Housing, health, everything like that.
Now the brief's changed and the pūtea is not so much.
But given Māori are so much under attack, shouldn't the minister, who-- I don't
know who I'm asking this question to.
Is it you, Tama, or is it-
It's Dave's turn.
Is it to Dave? Right. Given that Māori are so much under
attack, is it right that TPK takes a cut?
I would say that principally, ethically, it is not right, given Māori are
the most under attack group right now in this country.
Given TPK is the most under-resourced, underfunded government
department, why on earth hasn't the minister gone in and said, "No, I'm not going
to take a cut"? It's not right.
I can't answer that question, but I can answer the first question.
Is that okay-
Absolutely
... Mr. Chair? Okay.
Well, he couldn't answer the first question, so you're in good company.
No. The first-
... question you asked was really about leading.
Te Puni Kōkiri has five core functions.
One of them is to lead.
Mm.
And it's to lead policy advice, and the advice in terms of
relationships between iwi and Māori and the Crown.
That's a leadership function. Under the Act, Te Puni Kōkiri
has the responsibility to promote achievement.
We do that under Section 5 of the Act.
Secondly, Te Puni Kōkiri has a responsibility to monitor.
Mm.
So we have to be clear about what our functions are.
Monitor the government departments?
Yeah.
Right.
Yeah. Now, what you've talked about in terms of health,
jobs, homes, there are agencies
responsible for leading those.
Not Te Puni Kōkiri.
But your job is to monitor them, right?
That's correct.
Mm.
So by-
Are they failing Māori at the moment, Dave?
Well, we can go into that, but let me go,
stay on point here, because I think it is a fundamental point.
Mm-hmm.
In November, your own report in November
Mm
identified that Te Puni Kōkiri should align its strategic
intentions to Section 5 of the Act. Your report.
The Select-
Yeah
... Committee's report said that.
We launched the strategic intentions document prior to that report
that did exactly that, and I've reflected on those five
functions that are in our strategic intentions document.
So those are our five functions. That's what we do.
We don't lead in health. We don't lead in
jobs. We don't lead in homes. But we hold
them to account. It's really important because if we try and do
that, given the size of Te Puni Kōkiri, and the
fact that the expertise lies over in those mainstream
agencies with the resources-
... we will have to sacrifice something else, because those are big jobs.
They're responsible for delivering to all New Zealanders-
... including Māori.
And
to go to your second question, which is a policy
question on what the functions are of Te Puni Kōkiri,
until that changes, I'll do
what I'm mandated to do under legislation
and under Cabinet
and under the direction of the minister,
and that's appropriate. And that's what I'm funded to do.
Those are our core functions
I think-
So I think-
Oh, go ahead.
So just given that I come back to the question, the minister gave away $190
million in the last three years in terms of
TPK. Minister, the funding's gone down by that
over the last three years.
But some of that was time limited. It was sugar rushed in the previous government.
But
the minister has failed us in that area.
We haven't had one extra cent from this minister for
his department.
No, that's not true. That is blatantly untrue.
Well, let's say no, that's what the minister-
We've just got a tech contingency
for broadcasting, right? And we know that there was sugar rush money that
was not baseline.
We are seeking to ensure, as Te Puni Kōkiri, that we get clarity on how
that tech contingency will land, and I'm hoping, and let's see where this
lands, that some of that gets baseline.
Now, we also have had to ensure that
additional matters like the Nelson tents, like
the Māori Wardens, like the Māori Women's Welfare League,
like a couple of other items, have been given appropriate
scaffolding and support. Now, you talk about $190 million,
and I appreciate that, but some of that was time limited, like the Kaitaia Airport,
for example. That was a one-off batch of money to help fix that
place. And fortunately, it was preserved with the
advocacy of Te Puni Kōkiri because we know of the importance of
air and travel into Kaitaia when the Mangamuka
Gorge breaks and the Kaeo Bridge breaks, and those two things break all the
time. So you've got to have that access, and one of the reasons why we went hard on
that was because we knew that the Kaitaia Airport, by way of
treaty settlements, would ultimately be returned to the
hapū and the iwi there, which was done under our leadership and Dave's
support. So some of that money was time limited,
and I think it's important to delineate that.
Admittedly, some of that has come off as a result of budget
cuts and pressures, which many, many agencies have had to share.
Would I like
more resource to be in Te Puni Kōkiri? Yes.
Do I advocate for that? Yes. Do I think that Whānau Ora should get more money
in the future? Absolutely yes. But let's make sure,
as from yesterday's discussion, that system is really credible,
data-driven, evidenced, and functional, and then we will have a
more powerful argument to go and advocate for more pūtea.
Now-
Orangi.
Mōrena. Te Minita.
A key word that you mentioned, time-limited funding.
Yes.
And that's been quite a topic in our select committee
hui over the last few months. The question is, to what extent
does reliance on that time-limited funding actually undermine the
sustainability of Māori development services?
Well, I think there's time-limited funding across a range of agencies, some of
which do have major influence over outcomes for Māori and outcomes
for iwi. So I don't think it's restricted just to Te Puni Kōkiri, the
time-limited funding. For example, the Jobs for Nature funding.
That was time-limited funding. That was huge money that was put into Kai
MahI Putataio. And some of those projects I think were really, really
credible and positive and engaged and employed a lot of our
whānau. Then some of those other projects, they never really work.
Mm.
And
I sort of have some experience because I was involved in
running one of those Jobs for Nature projects out in the Hauraki Gulf.
So do I
think that time-limited funding is a good enduring way to
support some things? No. But is it appropriate sometimes? Yes.
Sometimes it is appropriate because there might be a specific project or might be a
specific capital investment that is required to be made where
time-limited funding does work. I think what I
might observe is that in terms of those four
areas that we're sort of describing today,
I would certainly like, where credible, more pūtea to be allocated.
Whatever the case, Te Puni Kōkiri has to hold the
99.5% of pūtea in the budget that does not come
to Te Puni Kōkiri to account for
delivering on Māori outcomes.
And can that be better? Absolutely.
Supplementary. Sorry, just-
Go ahead, yeah
... just one.
If I can just add to that, some of that is for a reason.
Kaitaia Airport-
Yeah
... was completed,
and some of that was for weather events-
Yeah
... that happened at the time. And so those were
time limited.
Supplementary, Mr. Chair.
Supplementary, yeah.
Delise.
Baseline funding. Are you committed to lifting baseline funding, knowing
that many of the entities in the kaupapa that have
existed under the time-limited funding still want to
exist and still have relevancy in today's modern society?
We're talking-- I'm not just referring to widespread.
I am also of the broadcast
collective as well. But
where time-limited funding must expire, baseline.
Are you committed to lifting the baseline funding across?
Well, I'm committed to advocating for that.
That's not a decision that I alone can make.
And that advocacy is something that
both Dave and the Ministry of Māori Development and I continue to
pursue.
But we do not ultimately,
as Te Mana Ō Te Tai, control budget.
That's something that we have to advocate for and work on.
Do you agree that it must be lifted?
Well, I think we have to make sure that we use the funding, the budget
appropriation that we have in a very prudent and fiscally responsible way.
And I think that there are some areas where I'm sure in the
next budget round, wherever we may be politically,
that will continue to be advocacy on my part for some of the funding to be lifted
in some areas. After yesterday's discussion, I
think you've got a sense that I'm pretty committed to ensuring and supporting
Whānau Ora to be successful. And if it is successful, then the
advocacy should be strong from all of us to lift the funding for Whānau Ora.
Yes, Dave.
As a general rule, we operate
both non-departmental and departmental funding.
And as a general rule, we work between 14 and 16% for
departmental, and the remainder is non-departmental.
And non-departmental goes out into the communities, the
broadcasting sector, and to whānau ora.
But when we
are managing
limited resources, we do prioritize what is
going out
as opposed to the organization.
Mm-hmm.
Currently, we operate at 16%.
So most of the funding that goes through Te Puni Kōkiri is like Postbox.
It goes through Te Puni Kōkiri and out to community entities or
businesses, Māori businesses, or the broadcasting entities.
It doesn't just stay in Te Puni Kōkiri. I think it's 16%, isn't it?
Yeah.
Stays within Te Puni Kōkiri, and the other 84 is out to others.
And that sort of aligns with our view, locally led, whānau centered, government
